Author 1 comment: A few errors in the R syntax. For example, the term "MCDATA" was not supposed to be in quotation marks because doing so passes it as a character string rather than as the actual dataset object, which causes functions like filter() to fail. Also, the values came back the other way around (e.g. Mdifference = -0.9, 95% CI [-1.15, -0.66]) where it was supposed to be Mdifference = 0.90, 95% CI [0.66, 1.15]. This is because the paired t-test was computed as pretest minus posttest, rather than posttest minus pretest, which flips the direction of the mean difference. B ut at the end, got it to reproduce.

I followed the paper’s design by creating Highmag, presencequize, and Group from V6 to run a 2×2×2 MANOVA on seven teaching effectiveness items. At first, I mistakenly filtered by METADATA, thinking it referred to participant status, but later realized it indicated Experiment 1 vs. 2. I corrected this and used the actual status variable for faculty vs. student. The main issue I encountered was missing data—while most DVs had 213 participants, two variables (helpful and challenging) only had data for 150, which reduced power and led to a smaller F and ηp² than reported. I tried various GLM combinations and confirmed that this missingness was the cause. CHATGPT suggested removing a part of the 2x2x2 to make it 2x2 (removing participant statust) but that is incorrect and not a solution. Also, the authors did not specify how I should deal with any missing data (e.g. use means).
Author 2 comment: The condition variable needs to be split into two variables, which can then be used in a 2 x 2 x 2 MANOVA with the 7 teaching evaluation variables as DVs (after first selecting only Experiment = 1.

Unsure of which variables represented “Interest” because the dataset didn’t include variable labels, and the paper didn’t clearly map items to constructs. I tried several combinations of variables that looked like Likert scales, but none aligned with the reported statistics. Eventually, I settled on v_31 to v_34 based on their scale range and internal consistency. They seemed to match a 4-item interest measure. Even though the df didn’t match the paper (they report df = 334 and I got 362), the result structure was close, and the domain × priming interaction was also similar in pattern. I still believe this dataset is unusually difficult to reproduce due to missing labels and unclear item mappings, but this attempt got me the closest to their reported Interest results.
Author 2 comment: This one is a real head scratcher. The variables with all the -77s on them appear to distinguish between the 5 priming conditions. And then control participants are presumably people who don’t have anything other than -77s between v_66 and v_81. I’d say that interest in the four domains is captured in v_31 to v_34, but again this is mostly a reasonable guess based on the info in the online supplements. They say that there were two types of attention checks, and I think that these are V_78 to v_81 (for the five priming conditions) plus v_82 (for everyone). They also say that 28 people were excluded based on failing these plus some other things (e.g., “seriousness check”). If I exclude based on both types of attention check (which the paper seems to imply), I lose way too many people. If I exclude based on one or the other, I don’t lose enough. And this is, of course, assuming that I’m even picking the correct items and then handing them in the ways that I think I am! I’ve attached as far as I got. I did the sorting into priming conditions manually, so there is no code for this. This one is a mess.
